| Factors affecting the urban heat island intensity  * Anthropogenic heat from air conditioning, cars, etc. increases the city temperature. * Closely spaced buildings, building materials and other urban surfaces (asphault, concrete, etc) store more heat during the day. * High wind speeds reduce the heat island, while low wind speeds or stagnant conditions allow for a greater heat island. * High albedo from cloud cover, snow cover, surface materials, etc. reduces the heat island by reflecting incoming radiation. * Drought conditions and low soil moisture increase the magnitude of the heat island. |
| While there are many factors that affect the formation and intensity of the urban heat island that can vary from city to city, the urban surfaces have the most dramatic influence on its magnitude. The heat island is known to exhibit its greatest magnitude at night especially during light winds and clear skies (Figuerola and Mazzeo, 1998). Using statistical analysis, the difference between the urban and rural stations was found to be greatest at 9pm, so this was the hour used to further analyze the spatial properties of the Dallas-Fort Worth urban heat island. |

# Urban Heat Island Research Project

### Background

In this project, we investigate the urban heat island (UHI) of the Dallas-Forth Worth metropolitan area. The urban heat island is the characteristic warmth of urban areas compared to their (non-urbanized) surroundings (Voogt, 2003). This phenomenon was first observed by Luke Howard (Howard, 1833), who took detailed temperature measurements in London from 1801 to 1841. While heat islands vary diurnally, seasonally, and in magnitude in different regions of the world, they generally follow a basic structural pattern. The urban heat island peaks over the most densely urbanized areas, with a lower magnitude over suburban and rural areas and a slight shift downwind.

![](Images/Idealized_UHI.png)

Heat islands are caused by urbanization, when buildings, roads and paved surfaces store the sensible heat during the day and then release it slowly during the evening, keeping urban lands hotter than surrounding areas. (Jusuf et. al, 2007). This results in a diurnal cycle in which the cities remain hotter after sunset, but take longer than the rural areas to heat during the day, sometimes resulting in a "cool island" around mid-day.

### Factors affecting the urban heat island intensity

While there are many factors that affect the formation and intensity of the urban heat island that can vary from city to city, the urban surfaces have the most dramatic influence on its magnitude. This is due to the greater daytime heat storage of urban areas due to the thermal conductivity of building and construction materials (Winguth and Clark, 2013). Anthropogenic heat release from building sides, traffic, air-conditioning during summer, and other electrical consumption (Sailor and Lu, 2004) also increases the urban heat island.

### The Dallas-Fort Worth urban heat island

Hourly data collected from air Continual Ambient Monitoring Stations (CAMS) implemented by the Texas Commission for Environmental Quality (TCEQ) were used in the Winguth and Clark (submitted) study. The magnitude of the heat island is calculated from the temperature difference between the urban station (downtown Dallas, CAMS 60) and the rural station (Kaufman, CAMS 71), which is located about 58 km downwind of the Dallas station (Winguth and Clark, submitted). The heat island is known to exhibit its greatest magnitude at night especially during light winds and clear skies (Figuerola and Mazzeo, 1998). Using statistical analysis, the difference between the urban and rural stations was found to be greatest at 9pm, so this was the hour used to further analyze the spatial properties of the Dallas-Fort Worth urban heat island.

![](Images/TCEQJuly2011_9pm.png)

In July 2011, there was a ~3.4 °C total change between Kaufman (CAMS 71) and downtown Dallas (CAMS 60), with light south to southeast winds of about 1.9 m/s which advect the warm temperatures northward. This was compared to satellite imagery at the end of July 2011 from the from MODIS/Terra satellite. Land cover data using vegetation types from the International Biosphere Geosphere Program (top figure below) coupled with nighttime surface temperature data (bottom figure below) support the northward transport of the heat island (Winguth and Clark, 2013).

![](Images/MODIS_Land_Use.png)
![](Images/Sat_Temp.png)

#### References Cited

![](PDF.png)

--- SOURCE 2: Urban heat island - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urban_heat_island

SUMMARY:
A definition of urban heat island is: "The relative warmth of a city compared with surrounding rural areas.": 2926 This relative warmth is caused by "heat trapping due to land use, the configuration and design of the built environment, including street layout and building size, the heat-absorbing properties of urban building materials, reduced ventilation, reduced greenery and water features, and domestic and industrial heat emissions generated directly from human activities".: 2926 The report goes on to say: "In a warming world, increasing air temperature makes the urban heat island effect in cities worse. Strategies to improve urban resilience by reducing excessive heat in cities include: Planting trees in cities, cool roofs (painted white or with reflective coating) "Reflective surfaces (climate engineering)") and light-coloured concrete, green infrastructure (including green roofs), passive daytime radiative cooling.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Urban heat island

[Urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") usually experience the **urban heat island** (**UHI**) effect, that is, they are significantly warmer than surrounding [rural areas](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area"). The [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature") difference is usually larger at night than during the day,[[1]](#cite_note-Phelan-1) and is most apparent when [winds](/wiki/Wind "Wind") are weak, under [block](/wiki/Block_(meteorology) "Block (meteorology)") conditions, noticeably during the [summer](/wiki/Summer "Summer") and [winter](/wiki/Winter "Winter").
The main cause of the UHI effect is from the modification of land surfaces, while [waste heat](/wiki/Waste_heat "Waste heat") generated by energy usage is a secondary contributor.[[2]](#cite_note-dx.doi.org-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-liyan2012-4) Urban areas occupy about 0.5% of the Earth's land surface but host more than half of the world's population.[[5]](#cite_note-5) As a population center grows, it tends to expand its area and increase its average temperature. The term **heat island** is also used; the term can be used to refer to any area that is relatively hotter than the surrounding, but generally refers to human-disturbed areas.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Milan_skyscrapers_aerial_view.jpg/330px-Milan_skyscrapers_aerial_view.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/New_York_City-Manhattan-Central_Park_%28Gentry%29.jpg/330px-New_York_City-Manhattan-Central_Park_%28Gentry%29.jpg)

Monthly [rainfall](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") is greater downwind of cities, partially due to the UHI. Increases in heat within urban centers increases the length of [growing seasons](/wiki/Growing_season "Growing season") and decreases the occurrence of weak [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"). The UHI decreases [air quality](/wiki/Air_quality "Air quality") by increasing the production of pollutants such as [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone"), and decreases water quality as warmer waters flow into area streams and put stress on their [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem").

Not all cities have a distinct urban heat island, and the heat island characteristics depend strongly on the background climate of the area in which the city is located.[[7]](#cite_note-SUE-7) The impact in a city can change a lot based on its local environment. Heat can be reduced by tree cover and green space which act as sources of shade and promote [evaporative](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration") cooling.[[8]](#cite_note-Waldrop-8) Other options include [green roofs](/wiki/Green_roof "Green roof"), [passive daytime radiative cooling](/wiki/Passive_daytime_radiative_cooling "Passive daytime radiative cooling") applications, and the [use of lighter-colored surfaces](/wiki/Reflective_surfaces_(climate_engineering) "Reflective surfaces (climate engineering)") and less absorptive building materials. These reflect more sunlight and absorb less heat.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:13-11)

[Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") is not the cause of urban heat islands but it is causing more frequent and more intense [heat waves](/wiki/Heat_wave "Heat wave") which in turn amplify the urban heat island effect in cities (see [climate change and cities](/wiki/Climate_change_and_cities "Climate change and cities")).[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12): 993 Compact, dense [urban development](/wiki/Urban_Development "Urban Development") may increase the urban heat island effect, leading to higher temperatures and increased exposure.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Contents

## Definition

A definition of *urban heat island* is: "The relative warmth of a city compared with surrounding rural areas."[[14]](#cite_note-:4-14): 2926 This relative warmth is caused by "heat trapping due to land use, the configuration and design of the [built environment](/wiki/Built_environment "Built environment"), including street layout and building size, the heat-absorbing properties of urban building materials, reduced ventilation, reduced greenery and water features, and domestic and industrial heat emissions generated directly from human activities".[[14]](#cite_note-:4-14): 2926

## Description

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Urban_heat_island.svg/500px-Urban_heat_island.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/HeatIsland_Kanto_en.png/500px-HeatIsland_Kanto_en.png)

### Diurnal variability

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Urban_heat_island_variation.jpg/500px-Urban_heat_island_variation.jpg)

Throughout the daytime, particularly when the skies are cloudless, urban surfaces are warmed by the absorption of [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation"). Surfaces in the urban areas tend to warm faster than those of the surrounding rural areas. By virtue of their high [heat capacities](/wiki/Heat_capacity "Heat capacity"), urban surfaces act as a reservoir of heat energy. For example, concrete can hold roughly 2,000 times as much heat as an equivalent volume of air.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As a result, high daytime surface temperatures within the UHI can be easily seen via thermal [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing"). As is often the case with daytime heating, this warming also has the effect of generating [convective](/wiki/Atmospheric_convection "Atmospheric convection") winds within the urban [boundary layer](/wiki/Planetary_boundary_layer "Planetary boundary layer"). At night, the situation reverses. The absence of solar heating leads to the decrease of atmospheric convection and the stabilization of urban boundary layer. If enough stabilization occurs, an [inversion layer](/wiki/Inversion_(meteorology) "Inversion (meteorology)") is formed. This traps urban air near the surface, keeping surface air warm from the still-warm urban surfaces, resulting in warmer nighttime air temperatures within the UHI.

Generally speaking, the difference in temperature between the urban and surrounding rural area is more pronounced at night than in daytime.[[15]](#cite_note-15) For example, in the United States, the temperature in urban areas tends to be warmer than the surrounding area by about 1–7 °F (0.55–3.9 °C) during the daytime, and about 2–5 °F (1.1–2.8 °C) warmer at night.[[16]](#cite_note-:22-16) However, the difference is more pronounced during the day in arid climates such as those in southeastern China and Taiwan.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Studies have shown that diurnal variability is impacted by several factors including local climate and weather, seasonality, humidity, vegetation, surfaces, and materials in the built environment.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[16]](#cite_note-:22-16)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Seasonal variability

Seasonal variability is less well understood than diurnal variability of the urban heat island temperature difference.[[22]](#cite_note-:8-22) Complex relationships between precipitation, vegetation, solar radiation, and surface materials in various local climate zones play interlocking roles that influence seasonal patterns of temperature variation in a particular urban heat island.[[22]](#cite_note-:8-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Measurements and predictions

### Urban Heat Island Index (UHII)

One method to quantify the UHI effect within urban areas is the UHI Index created by the [Californian EPA](/wiki/California_Environmental_Protection_Agency "California Environmental Protection Agency") in 2015. It compares the temperature of a surveyed area and rural reference points upwind from the surveyed area, at a height of two meters above ground level. The difference in temperature in degrees [Celsius](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") is taken hourly and differences with an increased urban temperature compared to the reference points are summed up, creating an amount of degree-Celsius-hours, which is the UHI Index of the surveyed area. The measure of Celsius-hours might be averaged over many days, but is specified as Celsius-hours per averaged day.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-:6-27)[[28]](#cite_note-:7-28)

The index was created to estimate the expected use of air conditioning and resulting [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") in California.[[27]](#cite_note-:6-27) The index does not consider values of or differences in wind-speed, humidity, or [solar influx](/wiki/Solar_irradiance "Solar irradiance"), which might influence [perceived temperature](/wiki/Apparent_temperature "Apparent temperature") or the operation of air conditioners.[[28]](#cite_note-:7-28)

### Models and simulations

If a city or town has a good system of taking weather observations the UHI can be measured directly.[[29]](#cite_note-29) An alternative is to use a complex simulation of the location to calculate the UHI, or to use an approximate empirical method.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) Such models allow the UHI to be included in estimates of future temperatures rises within cities due to climate change.

Leonard O. Myrup published the first comprehensive numerical treatment to predict the effects of the urban heat island (UHI) in 1969.[[32]](#cite_note-:3-32) The heat island effect was found to be the net result of several competing physical processes. In general, reduced evaporation in the city center and the thermal properties of the city building and paving materials are the dominant parameters.[[32]](#cite_note-:3-32) Modern simulation environments include [ENVI-met](/wiki/ENVI-met "ENVI-met"), which simulates all interactions between building and ground surfaces, plants and ambient air.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Causes

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/High-rise_buildings_of_Manhattan_during_sunset.jpg/250px-High-rise_buildings_of_Manhattan_during_sunset.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Newyork_heat_island.jpg/250px-Newyork_heat_island.jpg)

### Urban design

There are several causes of an urban heat island (UHI) related to common [urban design](/wiki/Urban_design "Urban design") aspects. For example, dark surfaces absorb significantly more [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation"), which causes urban concentrations of roads and buildings to heat more than suburban and rural areas during the day;[[2]](#cite_note-dx.doi.org-2) materials commonly used in urban areas for pavement and roofs, such as [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete") and [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete"), have significantly different thermal bulk properties (including [heat capacity](/wiki/Specific_heat_capacity "Specific heat capacity") and [thermal conductivity](/wiki/Thermal_conductivity "Thermal conductivity")) and surface radiative properties ([albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo") and [emissivity](/wiki/Emissivity "Emissivity")) than the surrounding rural areas. This causes a change in the [energy budget](/wiki/Earth%27s_energy_budget "Earth's energy budget") of the urban area, often leading to higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas.[[34]](#cite_note-Okeljnljn-34)

[Pavements](/wiki/Pavers_(flooring) "Pavers (flooring)"), [parking lots](/wiki/Parking_lot "Parking lot"), roads or, more generally speaking [transport infrastructure](/wiki/Transport_infrastructure "Transport infrastructure"), contribute significantly to the urban heat island effect.[[35]](#cite_note-:5-35) For example, pavement infrastructure is a main contributor to urban heat during summer afternoons in [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), United States.[[35]](#cite_note-:5-35)

Another major reason is the lack of [evapotranspiration](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration") (for example, through lack of vegetation) in urban areas.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36) The U.S. Forest Service found in 2018 that cities in the United States are losing 36 million trees each year.[[37]](#cite_note-37) With a decreased amount of vegetation, cities also lose the shade and evaporative cooling effect of trees.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Other causes of a UHI are due to geometric effects. The tall buildings within many urban areas provide multiple surfaces for the reflection and absorption of sunlight, increasing the efficiency with which urban areas are heated. This is called the "[urban canyon effect](/wiki/Urban_canyon "Urban canyon")". Another effect of buildings is the blocking of wind, which also inhibits cooling by [convection](/wiki/Convection "Convection") and prevents pollutants from dissipating. Waste heat from automobiles, air conditioning, industry, and other sources also contributes to the UHI.[[4]](#cite_note-liyan2012-4)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Heat islands can be affected by proximity to different types of land cover, so that proximity to barren land causes urban land to become hotter and proximity to vegetation makes it cooler.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Air pollution

High levels of [air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") in urban areas can also increase the UHI, as many forms of pollution change the radiative properties of the atmosphere.[[34]](#cite_note-Okeljnljn-34) UHI not only raises urban temperatures but also increases ozone concentrations because ozone is a [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") whose formation will accelerate with the increase of temperature.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Climate change as an amplifier

Climate change is not a cause but an amplifier of the urban heat island effect. The [IPCC Sixth Assessment Report](/wiki/IPCC_Sixth_Assessment_Report "IPCC Sixth Assessment Report") from 2022 summarized the available research accordingly: "Climate change increases [heat stress](/wiki/Heat_stress "Heat stress") risks in cities [...] and amplifies the urban heat island across Asian cities at 1.5 °C and 2 °C warming levels, both substantially larger than under present climates [...]."[[44]](#cite_note-44): 66

The report goes on to say: "In a warming world, increasing air temperature makes the urban heat island effect in cities worse. One key risk is [heatwaves](/wiki/Heat_wave "Heat wave") in cities that are likely to affect half of the future global urban population, with negative impacts on human health and economic productivity."[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12): 993

There are unhelpful interactions between heat and built infrastructure: These interactions increase the risk of heat stress for people living in cities.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12): 993

## Impacts

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Dubai_skyline_2015_%28crop%29.jpg/250px-Dubai_skyline_2015_%28crop%29.jpg)

### On weather and climate

Aside from the effect on temperature, UHIs can produce secondary effects on local meteorology, including the altering of local wind patterns, the development of [clouds](/wiki/Cloud "Cloud") and [fog](/wiki/Fog "Fog"), the [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity"), and the rates of precipitation.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The extra heat provided by the UHI leads to greater upward motion, which can induce additional shower and thunderstorm activity. In addition, the UHI creates during the day a local low pressure area where relatively moist air from its rural surroundings converges, possibly leading to more favorable conditions for cloud formation.[[46]](#cite_note-Hee-46) Rainfall rates downwind of cities are increased between 48% and 116%. Partly as a result of this warming, monthly rainfall is about 28% greater between 20 and 40 miles (32 and 64 km) downwind of cities, compared with upwind.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Some cities show a total precipitation increase of 51%.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

One study concluded that cities change the climate in area two–four times larger than their own area.[[49]](#cite_note-49) One 1999 comparison between urban and rural areas proposed that urban heat island effects have little influence on [global mean temperature trends](/wiki/Instrumental_temperature_record "Instrumental temperature record").[[50]](#cite_note-50) Others suggested that urban heat islands affect global climate by impacting the jet stream.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### On human health

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Atlanta_thermal.jpg/330px-Atlanta_thermal.jpg)

UHIs have the potential to directly influence the health and welfare of urban residents. As UHIs are characterized by increased temperature, they can potentially increase the magnitude and duration of heat waves within cities. The number of individuals exposed to extreme temperatures is increased by the UHI-induced warming.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The nighttime effect of UHIs can be particularly harmful during a heat wave, as it deprives urban residents of the cool relief found in rural areas during the night.[[53]](#cite_note-Clarke-53)

Increased temperatures have been reported to cause [heat illnesses](/wiki/Heat_illness "Heat illness"), such as [heat stroke](/wiki/Heat_stroke "Heat stroke"), [heat exhaustion](/wiki/Heat_exhaustion "Heat exhaustion"), [heat syncope](/wiki/Heat_syncope "Heat syncope"), and [heat cramps](/wiki/Heat_cramps "Heat cramps").[[54]](#cite_note-Review1-54)

Extreme heat is the deadliest form of weather in the U.S. Per a study by Professor Terri Adams-Fuller, heat waves kill more people in the U.S. than hurricanes, floods, and tornadoes combined.[[55]](#cite_note-55) These heat illnesses are more common within medium-to-large metro areas than the rest of the U.S., largely in part due to UHIs. Heat illnesses can also be compounded when combined with air pollution which is common in many urban areas.

Heat exposure can have adverse effects on mental health. Increases in temperature can contribute to increased aggression, as well as more cases of domestic violence and substance abuse.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Greater heat can also negatively impact school performance and education. According to a study by Hyunkuk Cho of Yeungnam University, an increased number of days with extreme heat each year correlates to a decrease in student test scores.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

High UHI intensity correlates with increased concentrations of air pollutants that gathered at night, which can affect the next day's [air quality](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution").[[58]](#cite_note-DOE-58) These pollutants include [volatile organic compounds](/wiki/Volatile_organic_compounds "Volatile organic compounds"), [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide"), [nitrogen oxides](/wiki/Nitrogen_oxides "Nitrogen oxides"), and [particulate matter](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates").[[59]](#cite_note-WHO-59) The production of these pollutants combined with the higher temperatures in UHIs can quicken the production of [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone").[[58]](#cite_note-DOE-58) Ozone at surface level is considered to be a harmful pollutant.[[58]](#cite_note-DOE-58) Studies suggest that increased temperatures in UHIs can increase polluted days but also note that other factors (e.g. [air pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure"), [cloud cover](/wiki/Cloud_cover "Cloud cover"), [wind speed](/wiki/Wind_speed "Wind speed")) can also have an effect on pollution.[[58]](#cite_note-DOE-58)

Studies from [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") have found that areas of the city with poorer outdoor urban air ventilation tended to have stronger urban heat island effects[[60]](#cite_note-60) and had significantly higher all-cause mortality[[61]](#cite_note-61) compared to areas with better ventilation. Another study employing advanced statistical methods in Babol city, Iran, revealed a significant increase in Surface Urban Heat Island Intensity (SUHII) from 1985 to 2017, influenced by both geographic direction and time. This research, enhancing the understanding of SUHII's spatial and temporal variations, emphasizes the need for precise urban planning to mitigate the health impacts of urban heat islands.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Surface UHI's are more prominent during the day and are measured using the land surface temperature and remote sensing.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### On water bodies and aquatic organisms

UHIs also impair [water quality](/wiki/Water_quality "Water quality"). Hot pavement and rooftop surfaces transfer their excess heat to stormwater, which then drains into storm sewers and raises water temperatures as it is released into streams, rivers, ponds, and lakes. Additionally, increased urban water body temperatures lead to a [decrease in biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss") in the water.[[64]](#cite_note-64) For example, in August 2001, rains over [Cedar Rapids, Iowa](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids,_Iowa "Cedar Rapids, Iowa") led to a 10.5 °C (18.9 °F) rise in the nearby stream within one hour, resulting in a [fish kill](/wiki/Fish_kill "Fish kill") which affected an estimated 188 fish.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Since the temperature of the rain was comparatively cool, the deaths could be attributed to the hot pavement of the city. Similar events have been documented across the American Midwest, as well as Oregon and California.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Rapid temperature changes can be stressful to aquatic ecosystems.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

With the temperature of the nearby buildings sometimes reaching a difference of over 50 °F (28 °C) from the near-surface air temperature, precipitation warms rapidly, and run-off into nearby streams, lakes and rivers (or other bodies of water) to provide excessive [thermal pollution](/wiki/Thermal_pollution "Thermal pollution"). The increase in thermal pollution has the potential to increase water temperature by 20 to 30 °F (11 to 17 °C). This increase causes the fish species inhabiting the body of water to undergo thermal stress and shock due to the rapid change in temperature of their habitat.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

[Permeable pavements](/wiki/Permeable_paving "Permeable paving") may reduce these effects by [percolating](/wiki/Percolation "Percolation") water through the pavement into subsurface storage areas where it can be dissipated through absorption and evaporation.[[69]](#cite_note-coolpave-69)

### On animals

Species that are good at colonizing can use conditions provided by urban heat islands to thrive in regions outside of their normal range. Examples of this include the [grey-headed flying fox](/wiki/Grey-headed_flying_fox "Grey-headed flying fox") (*Pteropus poliocephalus*) and the [common house gecko](/wiki/Common_house_gecko "Common house gecko") (*Hemidactylus frenatus*).[[70]](#cite_note-Paige_S_2005-70) Grey-headed flying foxes, found in [Melbourne, Australia](/wiki/Melbourne,_Australia "Melbourne, Australia"), colonized urban habitats following the increase in temperatures there. Increased temperatures, causing warmer winter conditions, made the city more similar in climate to the more northerly wildland habitat of the species.

With temperate climates, urban heat islands will extend the growing season, therefore altering breeding strategies of inhabiting species.[[70]](#cite_note-Paige_S_2005-70) This can be best observed in the effects that urban heat islands have on water temperature (see [effects on water bodies](#on_water_bodies_and_aquatic_organisms)).

Urban heat islands caused by cities have altered the [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") process.[[70]](#cite_note-Paige_S_2005-70) Selective pressures like temporal variation in food, predation and water are relaxed causing a new set of selective forces to roll out. For example, within urban habitats, insects are more abundant than in rural areas. Insects are [ectotherms](/wiki/Ectotherms "Ectotherms"). This means that they depend on the temperature of the environment to control their body temperature, making the warmer climates of the city perfect for their ability to thrive. A study done in [Raleigh, North Carolina](/wiki/Raleigh,_North_Carolina "Raleigh, North Carolina") conducted on *[Parthenolecanium quercifex](/wiki/Parthenolecanium_quercifex "Parthenolecanium quercifex")* (oak scales), showed that this particular species preferred warmer climates and were therefore found in higher abundance in urban habitats than on [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") trees in rural habitats. Over time spent living in urban habitats, they have adapted to thrive in warmer climates than in cooler ones.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### On energy usage for cooling

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/NASA_SLC_Urban_Heat_Island_Effect_Roof.jpg/500px-NASA_SLC_Urban_Heat_Island_Effect_Roof.jpg)

Another consequence of urban heat islands is the increased energy required for [air conditioning](/wiki/Air_conditioning "Air conditioning") and [refrigeration](/wiki/Refrigeration "Refrigeration") in cities that are in comparatively hot climates. The heat island effect costs [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") about [US$](/wiki/US%24 "US$") 100 million per year in energy (in the year 2000).[[72]](#cite_note-72) Through the implementation of heat island reduction strategies, significant annual net energy savings have been calculated for northern locations such as Chicago, Salt Lake City, and Toronto.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Every year in the U.S. 15% of energy goes towards the [air conditioning](/wiki/Air_conditioning "Air conditioning") of buildings in these urban heat islands. It was reported in 1998 that "the air conditioning demand has risen 10% within the last 40 years."[[74]](#cite_note-Rosenfeld98-74)

Increases in air conditioning use also serve to worsen the effects of UHIs at night. While cooler nights are often a reprieve from heat waves during the day, the residual heat created by the use of air conditioning systems can lead to higher nighttime temperatures. According to a study by Professor Francisco Salamanca Palou and colleagues, this residual heat can cause nighttime increases of up to 1 °C in urban areas.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Increased energy use from air conditioners also contributes to carbon emissions, which doubly exacerbates the effects of UHIs.

## Options for reducing heat island effects

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ogr%C3%B3d_botaniczny_UMCS.png/250px-Ogr%C3%B3d_botaniczny_UMCS.png)

Strategies to improve [urban resilience](/wiki/Urban_resilience "Urban resilience") by reducing excessive heat in cities include: Planting trees in cities, [cool roofs (painted white or with reflective coating)](/wiki/Reflective_surfaces_(climate_engineering) "Reflective surfaces (climate engineering)") and light-coloured concrete, [green infrastructure](/wiki/Green_infrastructure "Green infrastructure") (including [green roofs](/wiki/Green_roof "Green roof")), [passive daytime radiative cooling](/wiki/Passive_daytime_radiative_cooling "Passive daytime radiative cooling").[[76]](#cite_note-auto-76)

The temperature difference between urban areas and the surrounding suburban or rural areas can be as much as 5 °C (9.0 °F). Nearly 40 percent of that increase is due to the prevalence of dark roofs, with the remainder coming from dark-coloured pavement and the declining presence of vegetation. The heat island effect can be counteracted slightly by using white or reflective materials to build houses, roofs, pavements, and roads, thus increasing the overall albedo of the city.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Concentric expansion of cities is unfavourable in terms of the urban heat island phenomenon. It is recommended to plan the development of cities in strips, consistent with the hydrographic network, taking into account green areas with various plant species.[[78]](#cite_note-78) In this way, it was planned to build urban settlements stretching over large areas, e.g. [Kielce](/wiki/Kielce "Kielce"), [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin "Szczecin") and [Gdynia](/wiki/Gdynia "Gdynia") in Poland, [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") in Denmark and [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel") in Germany.

### Planting trees in cities

Planting trees around the city can be another way of increasing albedo and decreasing the urban heat island effect. It is recommended to plant [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees because they can provide many benefits such as more shade in the summer and not blocking warmth in winter.[[79]](#cite_note-Rosenfield97-79) Trees are a necessary feature in combating most of the urban heat island effect because they reduce air temperatures by 10 °F (5.6 °C),[[80]](#cite_note-80) and surface temperatures by up to 20–45 °F (11–25 °C).[[81]](#cite_note-81) Another benefit of having trees in a city is that trees also help fight global warming by absorbing CO2 from the atmosphere.

### Cool roofs and light-coloured concrete

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/20080708_Chicago_City_Hall_Green_Roof.JPG/250px-20080708_Chicago_City_Hall_Green_Roof.JPG)

Painting rooftops white has become a common strategy to reduce the heat island effect.[[82]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-82) In cities, there are many dark coloured surfaces that absorb the heat of the sun in turn lowering the albedo of the city.[[82]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-82) White rooftops allow high solar reflectance and high solar emittance, increasing the albedo of the city or area the effect is occurring.[[82]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-82) Green roofs and cool roofs can play a big role in helping cities manage extreme heat. In warmer regions, cool roofs were especially effective, with some cutting cooling energy use completely for certa

enriched_points.expanded_content: Rapid urbanization transforms land surfaces, replacing vegetation with buildings and roads, which increases heat absorption and disrupts natural cooling processes, intensifying the Urban Heat Island effect.

Materials like asphalt and concrete have high thermal mass, absorbing and retaining heat during the day, then releasing it slowly at night, exacerbating temperature differences between urban and rural areas.
Human activities, including transportation and industrial processes, emit waste heat into the urban environment, further elevating temperatures and contributing to the Urban Heat Island phenomenon.
